steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures on each side of the transition
steps.content: The sentence has two parts: first independent clause: “Scholars wishing to read a work written by the Roman scholar needn't despair” and second independent clause: “they can still procure a copy of Varro's Rerum rusticarum libra tres, a treatise on agriculture.” The word “though” connects them as a transition showing contrast between the sad news in the first sentence (many works lost) and the reason not to despair (this one is still available).

steps.title: Apply the SAT punctuation rule for transition words between independent clauses
steps.content:
When two independent clauses are joined by a transition word like “though” positioned mid-sentence, the correct pattern is: comma before the transition, semicolon after (IC, transition; IC).

The semicolon after “though” marks a stronger break than a comma alone, appropriate for two closely related but distinct complete thoughts.

The comma-semicolon pairing shows both the pause at “though” and the relationship between the contrasting ideas.

Options A and B lack the semicolon needed after “though” to properly separate two independent clauses; option D, while stylistically emphatic, uses an em dash instead of the standard punctuation pattern the SAT tests.

That matches choice C — C is correct.
